name: Nevada
notes_normalized: Cannabis is legal in Nevada under state law for recreational adult use. Medical cannabis is also legal under a regulated state program in Nevada. Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States. Possession is limited to varies (see official sources).
notes_raw: style="background:#196E92;" | {{Hs|1}} Cannabis in Nevada became legal for recreational use on January 1, 2017, following the passage of Question 2 on the 2016 ballot with 54% of the vote. The first licensed sales of recreational cannabis began on July 1, 2017. The first medical cannabis dispensary opened on July 31, 2015. Nevada first banned cannabis in 1923, during a nationwide trend of states limiting the drug between 1911 and 1933. Nevada Medical Marijuana Act (1998, 2000). A ballot measure to legalize cannabis for medical use, the Nevada Medical Marijuana Act, passed with 59% of the vote in 1998. Prior to the passage of AB 453, Nevada was the only state for which possessing any amount of cannabis was a felony. Titled the Decriminalization of Marijuana Amendment, the measure would have legalized the possession of up to three ounces of cannabis and allowed it to be sold at dispensaries. Medical use was legalized after a pair of ballot measures passed in 1998 and 2000. Legislation to allow for licensed sales was approved separately in 2013. Prohibition (1923). Nevada first banned cannabis in 1923, during a nationwide trend of states limiting the drug between 1911 and 1933. Nevada Medical Marijuana Act (1998, 2000). A ballot measure to legalize cannabis for medical use, the Nevada Medical Marijuana Act, passed with 59% of the vote in 1998. It passed for a second time in 2000 with 65% of the vote. Medical use was officially legalized with the June 2001 passage of Assembly Bill 453, which took effect on October 1. Titled the Decriminalization of Marijuana Amendment, the measure would have legalized the possession of up to three ounces of cannabis and allowed it to be sold at dispensaries. A second initiative to legalize cannabis for recreational use, the Regulation of Marijuana Initiative (Question 7), failed with 44% of the vote in 2006. It would have legalized the possession of up to an ounce of cannabis and allowed it to be sold at dispensaries, while also increasing penalties for some cannabis offenses. Violators will be penalized with a $400 fine, 2 days in jail, 48 – 96 hours of community service to $5000 fine and 1 – 6 years in prison depending on the first, second and third offence. If no felony charges for distribution or trafficking of controlled substances, the applicant will be approved for their MMR card subject to further approval by the Division. Nevada Laws and Penalties (NORML) Nevada Division of Public and Behavioral Health Medical Marijuana Page Text of Question 2 that appeared on November 2016 ballot Titled the Decriminalization of Marijuana Amendment, the measure would have legalized the possession of up to three ounces of cannabis and allowed it to be sold at dispensaries. It would have legalized the possession of up to an ounce of cannabis and allowed it to be sold at dispensaries, while also increasing penalties for some cannabis offenses. If no felony charges for distribution or trafficking of controlled substances, the applicant will be approved for their MMR card subject to further approval by the Division. In June 2023, Governor Joe Lombardo signed into law a bill to increase possession limits from 1 ounce of flower to 2.5, and from 1/8 ounce of concentrate to 1/4, effective January 1, 2024. The state of Nevada conducted a public workshop on February 4, 2016, in order to solidify proposed changes to NRS 453 defining serving sizes for edible products containing THC as well as clarifying language within the statute. The initiative also allows adults to cultivate up to six plants if they live more than 25 miles from a dispensary. Personal cultivation of marijuana for private medicinal use was the only available means of consumption, with a maximum of 12 plants allowed. The first provisional certificates for legal commercial medical marijuana operations were limited to 372 businesses within the state: 182 for cultivation, 118 for production, 55 for dispensaries, and 17 for independent testing laboratories. As of February 3, 2016, there are 5 laboratories, 19 cultivation facilities, 8 production facilities, and 15 licensed dispensaries operating in Clark County. In November 2022, the Cannabis Compliance Board announced the issuance of 40 prospective licenses for consumption lounges. The first to open on non-tribal land was Smoke and Mirrors lounge operated by THRIVE Cannabis Marketplace in February 2024. Clark County Commissioner Tick Segerblom became the first customer to consume cannabis on-site when he lit up a joint at 4:20 pm on opening day. Personal cultivation of marijuana for private medicinal use was the only available means of consumption, with a maximum of 12 plants allowed. Consumption in a public place, at a detention or correctional facility, or during delivery of marijuana to another person is illegal. The process of obtaining a permit to consume medical marijuana in Nevada begins with the applicant paying the $25 application fee for their application packet.

state_modifiers.federal_conflict: Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States.
